coredata.dc:title: Phoenix dactylifera lignocellulosic biomass as precursor for nanostructure fabrication using integrated process
coredata.dc:creator: Athinarayanan, Jegan
Periasamy, Vaiyapuri Subbarayan
Alshatwi, Ali A.
coredata.dc:description: Agricultural residue is an inevitable byproduct of agricultural activities. Valorization of agricultural residue is necessary to avoid wastage. In this study, we developed an integrated process for fabricating various nanostructures using Phoenix dactylifera (PD) lignocellulosic biomass as a sustainable bioprecursor. The PD biomass was treated using a hydrothermal method at 180 °C to derive the liquid fraction containing carbon nanodots (CDs). After pretreatment, the biomass was treated with an alkali solution to disassociate the lignin and cellulose. The extracted lignin was dissolved in tetrahydrofuran and subjected to a dialysis process. During the dialysis process, the lignin transformed into lignin nanoparticles (LNPs). The cellulose-containing fraction was transformed into cellulose nanostructures (CNs) via a sequential process (bleaching and acid hydrolysis). The structural properties, morphological features, crystallinity, and thermal stability of the fabricated CDs, LNPs, and CNs were analyzed. The synthesized CDs and LNPs had a spherical shape with different sizes: 2–10 nm (CDs) and 100–900 nm (LNPs). The CNs exhibited a fibrillated architecture and were 5–10 nm wide and 400–700 nm long. A biocompatibility analysis indicated that the synthesized nanostructures are nontoxic to human mesenchymal stem cells at concentrations of up to 200 μg/mL. This process is suitable for synthesizing nanostructures using different types of plant lignocellulosic biomass.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phoenix dactylifera
Carbon nanodots
Lignin nanoparticles
Cellulose nanostructures

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of nanostructures from lignocellulosic biomass

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of integrated process for nanostructure fabrication from PD lignocellulosic biomass.
Digital photographs of PD lignocellulosic-derived products obtained via the integrated process.
FTIR spectra of the PD lignocellulosic biomass in each step of the integrated process: (a) raw, (b) after pretreatment, (c) after alkali treatment, and (d) after bleaching.
PD lignocellulosic biomass-derived CDs: (a) UV–vis spectra, (b) TEM image, (c, d) AFM images, (e) FTIR spectra, and (f) XRD pattern.
(a) FTIR spectra, (b) thermogravimetric analysis curves, and (d) FE-SEM images of LNPs from date-palm waste.
Synthesized nanofibrillated cellulose: (a) FTIR spectra, (b) XRD pattern, and (c, d) TEM images.

Biocompatibility assessment of PD lignocellulosic biomass-derived CDs, LNPs, and CNs using hMSCs as an in vitro model. After 24 and 48 h of exposure to CDs, LNPs, and CNs, the cell viability of the hMSCs was assessed via an MTT assay.
Morphological changes in hMSCs exposed to CDs, LNPs, and CNs for 24 h (400× microscope images). Fluorescence microscope images of hMSCs stained using acridine orange/ethidium bromide (AO/EB).
Functional groups of the PD lignocellulosic biomass in each step of the integrated process.

head.ce:title: Phoenix dactylifera lignocellulosic biomass as precursor for nanostructure fabrication using integrated process

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanobiotechnology and Molecular Biology Research Laboratory, Department of Food Science and Nutrition, College of Food and Agricultural Sciences, King Saud University, P.O. Box 2460, Riyadh 11451, Saudi Arabia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanobiotechnology and Molecular Biology Research Laboratory
Department of Food Science and Nutrition
College of Food and Agricultural Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanobiotechnology and Molecular Biology Research Laboratory, Department of Food Science and Nutrition, College of Food and Agricultural Sciences, King Saud University, P.O. Box 2460, Riyadh 11451, Kingdom of Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Agricultural residue is an inevitable byproduct of agricultural activities. Valorization of agricultural residue is necessary to avoid wastage. In this study, we developed an integrated process for fabricating various nanostructures using Phoenix dactylifera (PD) lignocellulosic biomass as a sustainable bioprecursor. The PD biomass was treated using a hydrothermal method at 180 °C to derive the liquid fraction containing carbon nanodots (CDs). After pretreatment, the biomass was treated with an alkali solution to disassociate the lignin and cellulose. The extracted lignin was dissolved in tetrahydrofuran and subjected to a dialysis process. During the dialysis process, the lignin transformed into lignin nanoparticles (LNPs). The cellulose-containing fraction was transformed into cellulose nanostructures (CNs) via a sequential process (bleaching and acid hydrolysis). The structural properties, morphological features, crystallinity, and thermal stability of the fabricated CDs, LNPs, and CNs were analyzed. The synthesized CDs and LNPs had a spherical shape with different sizes: 2–10 nm (CDs) and 100–900 nm (LNPs). The CNs exhibited a fibrillated architecture and were 5–10 nm wide and 400–700 nm long. A biocompatibility analysis indicated that the synthesized nanostructures are nontoxic to human mesenchymal stem cells at concentrations of up to 200 μg/mL. This process is suitable for synthesizing nanostructures using different types of plant lignocellulosic biomass.

ce:section.ce:para: Phoenix dactylifera L. (PD), which is commonly known as the date palm tree, belongs to the Arecaceae family and plays vital ecological and economic roles in Arab countries. Approximately 100 million PD trees grow worldwide, and 25% are cultivated in the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia [1]. The Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations stated that Arab countries harvest approximately 50% of dates worldwide [2,3]. Each PD tree generates approximately 40 kg of agricultural residue per year, including the leaves, spathes, sheaths, and petioles [4]. Because of the large number of date palm trees cultivated in Arab countries, a large amount of agricultural residue is produced each year [5]. A small portion of this residue is used for building construction, animal feed, fuel, shading, heating, crates, handicrafts, landfilling, and panel boards [6]. The remaining majority of date palm tree waste is burned, causing environmental contamination [4]. Thus, the environmental impact caused by date-palm residue must be minimized by developing new technologies that better utilize the residue.

Recently, nanostructured materials (NMs) have received considerable attention in the scientific community, as well as from the public. The shape, size, surface area, chemical composition, and aggregation state of NMs are based on their physical, chemical, and biological properties and greatly differ from those of bulk materials with the same composition [7,8]. Moreover, NMs have been used to improve the quality, efficiency, stability, and function of products in various sectors, including the textile, food, agriculture, information technology, electronics, energy, and medical industries [9–11]. In particular, NMs are promising and versatile tools for biomedical applications, e.g., antimicrobial agents, bioimaging contrast agents, tissue engineering, three-dimensional (3D) cell-culturing scaffolds, disease diagnosis and therapy, and drug and gene carriers [7–11]. High industrial demands have led to studies aimed at developing harmless, cost-effective, and ecofriendly approaches for producing such NMs. The main goal of green chemistry is to use sustainable, biodegradable, ecofriendly, and renewable feedstock to produce value-added products [12]. The fabrication of NMs from lignocellulosic agricultural residue has been examined by material scientists because of its wide availability, sustainability, renewability, and ecofriendly nature [13,14]. Few studies have been conducted on the synthesis of biomaterials such as cellulose, chitosan, and lignin from agro-waste [15–17]. Plant biomass is composed of biopolymers such as lignin, hemicellulose, and cellulose [18]. NMs have been synthesized through various methods using numerous nonrenewable precursors. Nonrenewable precursors have many disadvantages: they are expensive, rare, and hazardous [19]. Agricultural residue is renewable, sustainable, inexpensive, highly available, and biodegradable and contains various organic and inorganic minerals, including lignin, pectin, cellulose, hemicellulose, and silica [20]. Lignin and cellulose are the most abundant biopolymers worldwide. The lignin polymer is associated with hemicellulose and cellulose in plant biomass [21].

Lignin nanoparticles (LNPs) and cellulose nanostructures (CNs) have been used for various purposes because of their nonreactive nature. For instance, LNPs are employed as dispersants and sizing agents (in the paper industry), binders, agrochemical delivery agents, antioxidants, and ultraviolet (UV) protection agents [22]. Interestingly, CNs have been utilized for food packaging, flexible supercapacitors, hydrogels, scaffolds, sensors for waterborne pathogens, and water purification [23–26]. Similarly, zero-dimensional C nanodots (CDs) have received attention because of their fluorescence emission properties. In recent years, the demand for LNPs, CNs, and CDs has increased worldwide because of their wide variety of applications. Thus, in this study, we utilized PD lignocellulosic biomass as a sustainable precursor for fabricating CDs, LNPs, and CNs sequentially using an integrated process.
Date palm plant biomass was collected from a date farm in Riyadh, Kingdom of Saudi Arabia.

The collected plant biomass was washed with distilled water to eliminate unwanted dust and fine sands. The washed plant biomass was dried and powdered, and then 25 g/batch of plant biomass powder was mixed with 500 mL of distilled water and kept in a hydrothermal autoclave at 180 °C for 2 h [27]. The severity value of the pretreatment was 4.4. After the hydrothermal reaction, the obtained product was centrifuged at 20,000 rpm for 30 min. The supernatant (liquid fraction) was separated and filtered through a 0.2-μm sterile filter. The obtained CD solution was freeze-dried and used for further characterization. The remaining residue (solid fraction) was used for lignin and CN fabrication.

Lignin was extracted using a previously reported method, with slight modification [16]. The obtained solid residue was mixed with 5% sodium hydroxide and sterilized in a hydrothermal autoclave at 170 °C for 4 h. The obtained products were filtered through Whatman 1 filter paper. The solid fraction was used for CN fabrication, and the liquid fraction was used for LNP preparation. The pH of the liquid fraction was adjusted to 2 using diluted hydrochloric acid for lignin precipitation. The obtained precipitate was washed with distilled water. After washing, the lignin was dissolved in tetrahydrofuran and dialyzed. The obtained products were LNPs [28].

The remaining solid fraction from the previous step was bleached at 95 °C for 90 min using a bleaching solution [1:1 solution of acetate buffer (27 g NaOH and 75 mL acetic acid in 1 L water) and 1.7% sodium hypochlorite]. After bleaching, the obtained residue was washed until the pH was neutral. The obtained white cellulose material was dried at 60 °C. The cellulose was treated with 45% sulfuric acid at 45 °C for 25 min with stirring [15]. This mixture was incubated at −80 °C for 1 h. The product was washed with distilled water and centrifuged. The obtained residue was sonicated for 10 min and dialyzed. The obtained colloid was freeze-dried for further physicochemical characterization.

The crystalline nature and surface functional groups of the samples were analyzed using an X-ray diffractometer and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, respectively. The crystalline nature of the powder samples was evaluated using a Rigaku X-ray diffractometer (Tokyo, Japan; Cu Kα radiation = 1.5406 Å). The chemical functional groups of the dried solid samples were determined using FTIR spectroscopy (Bruker Vertex 70, Billerica, MA, USA). The optical properties of the CDs were analyzed using a UV–visible (vis)–near-infrared spectrophotometer (Vertex 5000). The thermal properties of the samples were investigated using a thermogravimetric analyzer. The morphological features of the prepared nanostructures were assessed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). For the TEM analysis, the nanostructures were glazed onto C-coated Cu grids, and the operation voltage was 200 kV.

Biocompatibility is an essential property of biologically applied materials [20]. We assessed the cytotoxic properties of the prepared CDs, LNPs, and CNs using human mesenchymal stem cells (hMSCs) as an in vitro model. The effects of the CDs, LNPs, and CNs on the cell viability of the hMSCs were examined using a previously reported method [29,30]. The cells were plated at 10,000 cells per well in 96-well plates and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. After incubation, the cells were treated with different concentrations (0, 12.5, 25, 50, 100, and 200 μg/mL) of synthesized nanostructures for 24 and 48 h. After the treatment, 20 μL of a 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide solution was added to each well, followed by incubation in the dark for 4 h at 37 °C. The obtained purple formazan crystals were dissolved in 100 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide per well, and the sample absorbance was measured using a microplate reader (Bio-Rad, Hercules, CA, USA) at 570 nm. Data were collected in triplicate for each concentration of materials, and the mean values were calculated.

hMSCs were seeded into six-well plates and pretreated with different concentrations (0, 25, 50, and 100 μg/mL) of prepared nanostructures for 24 h. The cellular and nuclear morphological changes were assessed using a previously reported method [31]. After incubation, the cellular morphology was observed using a bright-field microscope. For nuclear-morphology analysis, the cells were treated with acridine orange/ethidium bromide and observed using a fluorescence microscope.
The mean ± standard deviation (SD) of the acquired data was computed using Microsoft Office.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of nanostructures from lignocellulosic biomass
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, plant lignocellulosic biomass is mainly composed of organic substances such as hemicellulose, lignin, and cellulose. However, the composition of plant biomass varies among plants. Plant biomass is highly suitable for fabricating various materials. Date-tree biomass is primarily composed of cellulose (39%–47%) and lignin (32%–35%) at high concentrations, as well as hemicellulose (15%–25%). Interestingly, date-palm leaflets contain 41%–43% cellulose and 32%–36% lignin. Moreover, date-palm leaf stalks contain 44%–47% cellulose and 33%–38% lignin [5]. Compared with other parts of the date palm, the waste of leaflets and leaf stalks is abundant, and its cellulose and lignin contents are very high. Thus, date-palm biomass is suitable for fabricating cellulose, lignin, and C-based nanostructures. In the present study, we fabricated CDs, LNPs, and CNs from date-palm biomass using an integrated process.

Fig. 1 presents the integrated process of nanostructure fabrication. Pretreatment can be used to overcome the natural resistance of the date biomass structure [32]. For instance, the biomass can be pretreated with diluted acid, steam, or water to disintegrate the hemicellulose into sugar molecules. Additionally, pretreatment modifies the crystalline structure of the cellulose and allows admittance of enzymes for degradation [33]. In this study, date-tree biomass was pretreated with water at 180 °C for 2 h in a hydrothermal reactor. To simplify the pretreatment process, no chemicals or neutralization of the downstream process were used [33]. During the initial pretreatment stage, xylan, sugar molecules, and other substances were released from the plant biomass. Subsequently, the released xylan and sugar molecules were transformed into CDs at 180 °C under pressurized conditions. The hydrothermal treatment of biomass at 180 °C led to the formation of CDs [34]. After pretreatment, the liquid portion contained CDs, and the remaining solid residue mainly comprised lignin and cellulose. Sodium hydroxide treatment of lignocellulose at 60–180 °C efficiently destroyed the bonds between lignin and carbohydrate complexes [35]. Kim et al. suggested that alkali treatment can eliminate approximately 60%–80% of lignin and 50% of hemicellulose by dissolution or degradation from biomass [35].

In the present study, after pretreatment, the solid residue was treated with sodium hydroxide at 170 °C. The alkali treatment dissolved the lignin and hemicellulose from the pretreated solid residue. Lignin was present in the liquid fraction, and the remaining solid residue comprised cellulose and a very small amount of lignin. The lignin was isolated through acid precipitation and was used to fabricate LNPs. For the LNP fabrication, the obtained lignin was dissolved in tetrahydrofuran and dialyzed. The solid residue was bleached to remove the remaining residue of lignin and to purify the cellulose. Finally, the cellulose was transformed into CNs through acid hydrolysis [15]. Fig. 2 presents a digital photograph of the date-tree biomass-derived NMs obtained using the integrated process. This integrated process is suitable for transforming lignocellulosic biomass derived from different plants into nanoscale materials. Fig. 3 shows the FTIR spectra of the PD biomass in each step of the integrated process. The FTIR absorption bands and the corresponding functional groups are presented in Table 1 . The PD biomass in the raw state, after pretreatment, after alkali treatment, and after bleaching exhibited different O-containing functional groups, i.e., OH (3400–3300 cm−1), C–O–C (1200–1150 cm−1), and OH association (1110–1103 cm−1). The peak around 1258–1248 cm−1 was not observed after the bleaching process, owing to the reduced amount of hemicellulose in the biomass.

The prepared CD solution was light yellow in color and exhibited blue fluorescence under UV light (Fig. 4a). The UV–vis spectroscopy results exhibited a peak at 280 nm corresponding to the π–π* transition of the CO bond, indicating the presence of aromatic π orbitals in the prepared CDs.

The TEM image clearly revealed well-dispersed spherical dots that were 2–10 nm in size (Fig. 4b). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images suggested that the particle size of the CDs was 5–30 nm (Fig. 4c and d). The TEM and AFM results agree with the morphological features of the date-palm biomass-derived CDs. After the CD synthesis, the remaining solid fraction was used for lignin and cellulose extraction. The FTIR spectra of the CDs (Fig. 4e) exhibited a peak at approximately 3242 cm−1, which was attributed to the –OH stretching mode, whereas the absorption bands at 1592 and 1405 cm−1 suggested the presence of CC stretching and OH bending, respectively. We observed a band at 1068 cm−1, which was assigned to CO stretching and CO deformation. The FTIR spectra of the CDs confirmed that the CD surface was glazed with hydroxyl groups. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the CDs (Fig. 4f) exhibited a wide-ranging peak at the 2θ value of 22.5°, corresponding to the (002) plane, which indicates an interlayer d spacing of ∼0.35 nm. This is larger than the interlayer spacing of the bulk graphite (0.33 nm). Hence, the CDs may have had a turbostratic C structure [36]. These characterization results provide strong evidence that the liquid fraction contained CDs.

The physicochemical properties of the extracted lignin and LNPs were analyzed. Fig. 5a shows the FTIR spectra of the lignin and LNPs, which closely matched. The broad peak in the range of 3400–3300 cm−1 was attributed to hydroxyl groups of the aliphatic and phenolic structure. Interestingly, many peaks were observed between 800 and 1700 cm−1. The peak at 898 cm−1 corresponded to alkyl-substituted alkene. The peaks at 1044, 1113, 1156, 1215, and 1313 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching mode of C-O-C. A peak corresponding to the stretching mode of CC was observed at 1598 cm−1, indicating that the groups were associated with the aromatic ring. Peaks corresponding to the stretching mode of aromatic CC were observed at 1500–1552 cm−1. Additionally, a peak corresponding to the bending scissoring mode of CH2 was observed at 1453 cm−1. The FTIR spectra peaks were characteristic of lignin.

Lignin is an organic material composed mostly of aromatic rings with various branches. The thermal behavior of the lignin and LNPs was studied, and the results are shown in Fig. 5b. Approximately 30% of the LNPs were un-volatized at 600 °C because of the formation of highly condensed aromatic structures that formed char. Moreover, the LNP degradation was divided into three stages. In the initial stage, water molecules were evaporated at 100–120 °C. In the second stage, the carbohydrates in the LNPs were degraded at approximately 175–325 °C. The third stage of degradation involved the production of volatile products from the LNPs. Thus, when the temperature was increased, the degradation processes were initiated, and the structure was damaged. The synthesized LNPs were more thermally stable than the lignin. The morphology of the LNPs was investigated using field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) (Fig. 5c and d). The images indicate that the LNPs were spherical and had diameters of 100–900 nm.

The FTIR spectra of the CNs (Fig. 6a) exhibited absorption bands at approximately 1427, 1315, and 1027 cm−1 , which were assigned to CH2 bending, CH2 rocking, and CO stretching, respectively. Additionally, the intense band at 897 cm−1 corresponded to the anomeric C group in cellulose [15]. The crystallinity of the fabricated CNs was analyzed using an X-ray diffractometer (Fig. 6b). The diffractograms exhibited peaks at 2θ = 14.9° and 21.98°, corresponding to the (101) and (002) planes, respectively, of the cellulose I structure (Fig. 6b). No other impurities were observed. The results agree with previous reports [15]. The structure and morphological features of the nanofibrillated cellulose were investigated using TEM (Fig. 6c and d). The fabricated CNs exhibited a fibrillated structure that was 5–10 nm wide and 400–700 nm long. The nanofibrils had an interconnected netting arrangement, which is highly suitable for biomedical applications. The results indicate that we successfully synthesized CDs, CNs, and LNPs from date-palm waste through the integrated process. This process can be applied for the large-scale production of nanostructures and for valorizing agro-waste. It can also be used for fabricating nanostructures from various types of plant biomass.

The biocompatibility of the synthesized materials was analyzed using hMSCs as an in vitro model. hMSCs have a capability of self-renewal and differentiate to various lineages, e.g., myocytic, chondrocytic, osteocytic, adipocytic, neurocytic, and hepatocytic [37–39]. The cell-viability results are shown in Fig. 7 .

The hMSCs were exposed to different concentrations of the prepared nanostructures (CDs, CNs, and LNPs). The CDs reduced the cell viability of the hMSCs at concentrations of approximately 7%–9% under 200-μg/mL treatment. The LNPs and CNs induced cell-viability reductions of 1%–2% and 6%–10%, respectively. However, no significant changes were observed in the hMSC cell viability. The cell-viability assay results clearly indicated that the PD biomass-derived nanostructures were nontoxic. Furthermore, we assessed the effect of the PD biomass-derived nanostructures on the cellular and nuclear morphology of hMSCs (Fig. 8 ). Compared with a control sample, the cells treated with the PD biomass-derived nanostructures were healthy. Additionally, fluorescence microscope images indicated intact healthy nuclei in the treated and untreated cells. These results indicate that the nanostructures did not modify the cellular and nuclear architecture of the hMSCs. Overall, the biocompatibility assay results suggested that the PD-derived nanostructures were nontoxic and can be used in various biomedical applications, such as scaffolds for 3D cell culturing, bioimaging, cancer therapy, and antioxidants.

We developed an integrated process for fabricating CDs, LNPs, and CNs from PD lignocellulosic biomass. CDs were synthesized via hydrothermal treatment of the PD biomass. Subsequently, the biomass was treated with an alkali solution to disassociate the lignin and cellulose. Furthermore, the lignin and cellulose were converted into LNPs and CNs, respectively. The structural and morphological features of the fabricated CDs, LNPs, and CNs were investigated. The synthesized nanostructures were spherical and exhibited different sizes: 2–10 nm for the CDs, 100–900 nm for the LNPs, and 5–10 nm (width) and 400–700 nm (length) for the CNs. The proposed process is applicable for the valorization of various types of lignocellulosic biomass. Additionally, the process is inexpensive, simple, and produces different products simultaneously. The fabricated nanostructures (CDs, LNPs, and CNs) are suitable for various applications, such as bioimaging, biosensing, scaffolds, drug delivery, food packaging materials, flexible sensors, and antioxidants. Thus, the synthesized materials are comparatively preferable as requires large quantity.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:section.ce:para: This work was supported by the Deanship of Scientific Research, King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia [Research Group Project no. RGP-1435-044]. The partial work of this manuscript has achieved a US patent (US patent granted on February 2018; US 9896342 B1).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Date fruit (Phoenix dactylifera L.): an underutilized food seeking industrial valorization

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Date palm tree (Phoenix dactylifera L.): natural products and therapeutic options

Aref, Chemical analysis of different parts of date palm (Phoenix dactylifera L.) using ultimate, proximate and thermo-gravimetric techniques for energy production. Energies. 9 (2016) 374.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Size and shape dependent second order nonlinear optical properties of nanomaterials and their application in biological and chemical sensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ray, P.C., 2010. Size and shape dependent second order nonlinear optical properties of nanomaterials and their application in biological and chemical sensing. Chem. Rev. 110 (2014) 5332–5365.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Chen, C. Ostrom, Palladium-based nanomaterials: synthesis and electrochemical applications. Chem. Rev. 115 (2015) 11999–12044.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanomaterials as enhanced antimicrobial agent/activity-enhancer for transdermal applications: a review

P. Anastas, N. Eghbali, Green chemistry: principles and practice. Chem. Soc. Rev. 39 (2010) 301–312.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synchronous and rapid preparation of lignin nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots from natural lignocellulose

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Si, J. Zhang, Y. He, Z. Yang, X. Yan, M. Liu, S. Zhuo, S. Wang, X. Min, C. Gao, L. Chai, Synchronous and rapid preparation of lignin nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots from natural lignocellulose. Green Chem. 20 (2018) 3414–3419.
R. Mohammadinejad, S. Karimi, S. Iravani, R.S. Varma, Plant-derived nanostructures: types and applications. Green Chem. 18 (2016) 20–52.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fabrication and cytotoxicity assessment of cellulose nanofibrils using Bassia eriophora biomass
